description: This chart presents the annual number of publications indexed in the Scopus bibliometric database for two related research topics from 2010 to 2022. Two curves are plotted: Light-weight steel (blue line) and Fe-Mn-Al-C steel (green line), with the y-axis showing search results in thousands. Both topics exhibit a continuous upward trend over the 13-year period. The Light-weight steel curve starts at approximately 13–14 thousand in 2010 and rises steeply to roughly 48 thousand by 2022. The Fe-Mn-Al-C steel curve begins at around 8 thousand in 2010 and reaches approximately 30 thousand in 2022. Throughout the entire period, the publication volume for Fe-Mn-Al-C steel remains below that of the broader Light-weight steel category, while both demonstrate accelerating growth after 2018. The figure supports the paper’s central argument that research interest in lightweight Fe-Mn-Al-C steels is continuously increasing, reflecting their growing relevance in automotive and structural applications for improved fuel efficiency and CO2 reduction.

This figure presents a hierarchical classification flowchart for Fe–Mn–Al–C lightweight steels, systematically dividing the alloy family into two primary categories based on composition. The left branch denotes multiphase Fe–Mn–Al–C steels with 5–12 wt.% Mn and ≤0.8 wt.% C, whereas the right branch denotes austenitic Fe–Mn–Al–C steels with >12 wt.% Mn and 0.8–1.2 wt.% C. Both branches feed into a central strength–ductility balance comparison: multiphase grades offer strength of 800–1200 MPa paired with 30–60% elongation, while austenitic grades span 600–1500 MPa in strength with elongation <60%. The diagram concludes by linking these property profiles to application areas—multiphase steels are directed toward automotive structural components and reinforcements, and austenitic steels toward automotive structural and military uses. The chart thus establishes a clear composition–microstructure–property–application relationship, supporting the conclusion that Mn and C levels dictate phase stability, mechanical performance windows, and end-use suitability.

Figure 3 presents calculated isothermal phase diagrams for Fe–20Mn–Al–C alloys at 900 °C (a) and 1200 °C (b), plotting weight percent Al (0–20 wt%) on the horizontal axis against weight percent C (0–5 wt%) on the vertical axis. Subfigure (a) at 900 °C displays solid-state phase fields including austenite (γ), ferrite (α), κ-carbide (κ), M₃C, Al₄C₃, and graphite (gra.), overlaid with experimental data points denoted by symbols indicating single-phase γ, two-phase γ+κ, α+γ, α+κ, and γ+M₃C equilibria. At this temperature, a broad γ+κ field dominates the intermediate Al–C regime, while M₃C and graphite become stable at higher carbon contents. Subfigure (b) at 1200 °C reveals the stabilization of a liquid (L) phase across much of the composition space, introducing γ+L and gra.+L fields while markedly shrinking the solid single-phase γ region and dissolving κ-carbide and M₃C phases. The figure demonstrates that increasing temperature from 900 °C to 1200 °C eliminates κ-carbide stability, promotes liquid formation, and alters austenite and ferrite boundary positions, providing essential thermodynamic guidance for alloy composition design and high-temperature processing windows in lightweight Fe–Mn–Al–C steels.

Figure 4 comprises two scatter-plot panels mapping yield strength (open symbols) and ultimate tensile strength (filled symbols) against elongation for lightweight Fe–Mn–Al–C steels at room temperature. Panel (a) covers alloys with average Mn contents (8, 10, 11, and 12 wt%), while panel (b) covers high-Mn steels grouped into four ranges (12–18, 18–25, 25–28, and 28–40 wt%). Symbol shapes encode C content from 0.5 up to >1.2 wt%. Panel (a) identifies the γ-based duplex (γ+α/δ) field, and panel (b) distinguishes both the γ-based duplex region and the single-γ austenitic domain. The data reveal that duplex grades tend to concentrate at moderate strength with lower elongation, whereas single-γ high-Mn steels achieve markedly higher strength–ductility combinations, with numerous data points surpassing 1000 MPa UTS alongside elongations extending toward 80%. These trends confirm that austenite stabilization via elevated Mn and C broadens the mechanical property window.

This figure integrates transmission electron microscopy, nanoscale elemental mapping, and mechanical data to demonstrate Si-regulated formation of κ-carbides in homogenized austenitic Fe–30Mn–9Al–1.2C steel. The leftmost TEM micrograph (50 nm scale bar) reveals the nanoscale precipitate morphology. The second panel shows an elemental distribution map (20 nm scale bar) overlaid for Fe, Al, Si, and C, where carbon-rich regions identify the κ-carbides. The central engineering stress–strain curves compare 0Si, 1Si, and 2Si alloys: increasing Si raises the yield strength from approximately 450 MPa to 950 MPa, while tensile ductility drops from roughly 80% to about 20% engineering strain. The rightmost nanoscale distribution map (25 nm scale bar) exhibits additional elemental contrast consistent with carbon-enriched carbide regions. Collectively, the panels establish that Si addition promotes intragranular cuboidal κ-carbides (L′1, average 11.5 nm, 25.9 vol% at 2 mass% Si) and larger intergranular κ-carbides (50–200 nm), delivering substantial precipitation strengthening at the expense of reduced uniform elongation.

Figure 6 presents four scanning electron microscopy-electron backscatter diffraction (SEM-EBSD) phase maps of centrifugally cast Fe–20Mn–9Al–3Cr–xC low-density steel strips with carbon contents of (a) 0.8C, (b) 1.0C, (c) 1.2C, and (d) 1.4C. In these maps, red contrast denotes ferrite and gray contrast denotes austenite, with black lines marking grain boundaries. As carbon content increases from 0.8 wt% to 1.4 wt%, the volume fraction of ferrite progressively decreases from a substantial area fraction in (a) to sparse, isolated islands in (d); caption states this corresponds to a reduction from 36 vol% to 4 vol%. Concurrently, the images visually reflect the microstructural transition accompanying increased carbon: ferrite is gradually replaced by austenite, and prior work reports that DO3 precipitation within ferrite diminishes while κ-carbide precipitation in austenite is promoted. This microstructural evolution correlates with mechanical property changes, where yield strength rises from 680 MPa to 992 MPa and elongation remains at least 33%. The figure supports the conclusion that when carbon exceeds 1.0 wt%, dispersion strengthening by κ-carbides becomes the dominant strengthening mechanism, increasing from 36.3 MPa to 409.4 MPa.

This figure displays electron probe microanalysis (EPMA) micrographs (a–d) and corresponding quantitative copper X-ray distribution maps (e–h) of Fe–28Mn–9Al–0.8C–3Cu (3Cu) strips in the as-cast state and after annealing at 500 °C, 600 °C, and 700 °C. The as-cast sample (a, e) contains large, irregular Cu-rich regions. As the annealing temperature increases (b–d, f–h), these large Cu-rich areas progressively diminish and fragment, while finer Cu-rich features become more dispersed within the matrix. The accompanying citation reports that the strips were annealed for 3 h and that Cu addition suppresses intergranular κ-carbide precipitation, contributing to increased strength. Specifically, the 600 °C annealed 3Cu strip is reported to achieve a yield strength of 653 MPa, an ultimate tensile strength of 965 MPa, and 25 % elongation. Overall, the figure demonstrates that elevating the annealing temperature refines the Cu-rich particle distribution, thereby modulating precipitate evolution and the mechanical property profile of the lightweight austenitic steel.

Figure 8 presents a temperature–time schematic illustrating the complete thermomechanical processing regimes for Fe–Mn–Al–C steel. The schedule begins with a high-temperature solution treatment at 1100–1200 °C, followed by multi-pass hot rolling, cooling, and subsequent cold rolling. After cold rolling, the diagram branches into four distinct downstream thermal routes: Route 1 (green arrow) applies annealing at 600–800 °C; Route 2 (red arrow) applies an annealing step also at 1100–1200 °C; Route 3 (blue arrow) applies aging at 450–700 °C; and Route 4 (purple dashed arrow) applies a variant aging treatment at 450–700 °C. These alternative post-deformation heat-treatment paths are designed to control the recovery, recrystallization, and precipitation kinetics—particularly of nano-sized κ-carbides—in the austenitic matrix, thereby enabling systematic tuning of the strength–ductility combination in lightweight Fe–Mn–Al–C steels. The caption states these regimes collectively represent the hot and cold rolling, annealing, and aging strategies adopted for this alloy system.
